reviews.author: GB
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 3, 2025
reviews.content: I buy and test a lot of phones to compare performance between brands and other general specifications. For under $100 this phone is a great value. The camera is only so so but perfectly functional - however people buying a phone at this price point probably aren’t concerned about top line picture quality. The call quality and battery life are excellent as is the 128 Gig of storage. The phones 1.6 GHz processor combined with four gigs of actual RAM and four gigs of additional virtual RAM move the phone along nicely for multitasking 3-4 tasks without issue. I really like the fact that the phone came with not only a charger and cable, but a functional screen protector and case. That alone saves $10-$15 in extra cost. Note this phone only supports T-Mobile and its resellers in the US. So no AT&T or Verizon. But if you want this for a backup phone or for a high school student it’s an excellent choice as lots of Tmobile resellers (like Tello and Metro by Tmobile) offer a range of cost-effective prepaid plans that won’t break the bank. The phone is 4G only but I had no problems getting a strong signal for calls and respectable download speeds. I think NUU is onto something with quality build low cost phones that meet a variety of consumer needs. I plan to buy a few other models of their phones to see if performance is just as consistent but I’m very pleased with this purchase. As a last point this phone is dual SIM, and also supports a microSD card to expand storage if you only use one SIM card.

reviews.author: Veronica
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 13, 2025
reviews.content:
I bought this phone as a back up phone and also as a travel phone for when I go to certain areas that aren't the safest places to be. It is dual sim so that when outside the country a local area sim card can be purchased and easily installed to accomadate your needs. I put a prepaid simple mobile sim card in it and it works just fine. It's not the fastest phone around but still does the job it needs to do. It comes with a screen cover pre installed and I like the flexible case it comes with. It doesn't have an esim feature but that usually isn't expected on a phone priced this low. As far as being a phone for a teenager, well......
(A) Teenagers need to do their homework and not be on the phone.
(B) They need to clean their room.
(C) They need to help out with the yard work.
Still this is a decent phone and can be used for just about anybody. A great burner, a back up phone that is good enough for those on a budget. It definetly deserves a good review!

reviews.author: Kraegor
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on November 29, 2025
reviews.content:
Okay so I have only had this phone a few days, but here is what I will say:

1) Build quality: Decent. Its actually a metal exoskeleton and feels more like a quality phone than most Samsungs. Its heavy and feels damn sturdy. I haven't dropped it but the aluminum siding feels great in the hands. The button responsiveness is okay as well.

2) Speed: I purchased this for an elderly person, so speed wasn't a number one priority. But after using this phone it feels fairly snappy and quick in the operating system. Videos play well, texting and other things work great. I am sure this isn't built for gaming or anything, buut for normal day to day use? Seems pretty good.

3) Price. You cannot beat $79 for a phone. I was expecting a piece of junk but this is easily better than some of the BLU phones I have tried that are 3-5 times the price.

4. Photos/Picture Qaulity: The screen quality is pretty good. Its not super high defintion, but for 1080p viewing and video its 100% fine. Photos taken are not iPhone or Samsung high end quality but with fairly good light they are pretty good. I wouldn't get this if you are wanting super high quality pictures, but for Instagram/Facebook posting? The photo quality is great.

My advice? If you have a young kid/teen or elderly person or just want a throw-around phone, get this. When a phone is this cheap, its easy to just replace instead of paying $100+ to get a screen repaired. As long as they aren't gaming, doing photo journalism or need to use the phone for 48 hours straight.. this is a great deal for the money.

reviews.author: Sad Day
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 30, 2026
reviews.content:
I was excited to find such a price conscience cell phone that was packed with everything I need in service. But with a heavy heart I must say if I could give this model N10 a ZERO STAR I would have. Reason is a MUST have in ANY cell phone it MUST BE capable of making calls!,
For two weeks now every time I try to make a call I get the message mobile network not available or the call does not connect period! To make it work I have to go into settings turn off both internet AND mobile network settings (I use Mint Mobile) often times I have to completely restart the phone.

So you tell me would this be the kind of phone you would waste your money on? NOPE I will be returning this paper weight in next couple days what a shame nice phone but can’t make calls ? Isn’t that the sole purpose of a cell phone? Keep your money !!! don’t through it away like I did.

reviews.author: Carla
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 26, 2026
reviews.content: Wow, I love this phone.... works great! Not to mention fun!

reviews.author: Ramin A.
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 13, 2026
reviews.content:
Great phone for the price but doesn't do everything I need unfortunately.

I may be spoiled by the Samsung Galaxy software and hardware but that is what I am comparing this with since I really wanted this phone as a back up phone for the family.

Pros:
1. price obviously
2. it functions with the google environment (installs apps from the playstore, etc.)
3. decent battery life (I easily got a day of use)
4. looks slick and great fit and finish
5. LTE /VoLTE 4 connects well and works

Cons:
1. camera is no good; most pictures come out very blurry and the resolution is low
2. screen is not as clear or bright as any Samsung phones I have had over the last 5-6 years
3. it's definitely slower than the newer phones from Samsung, just loading apps, text, etc. takes noticeably longer; forget about multi-tasking
4. speakers sound tinny
5. the UI is not as clean as Samsung's UI and some of the functionality only works at best 50% of the time, like extended unlock
6. the Bluetooth is not as good, it fails to connect to my car reliably (phone as a key function is also unreliable about 50% of the time)
7. gets weak signal in many places where my older Samsung phones work great, so must be something with the hardware
8. some apps won't install because playstore things the phone is not in the US
9. forget multi-tasking like streaming a podcast while doing other things - it unloads most apps from its RAM if you switch to another app, so 2-step authentication won't work either because the one you switched out of has to reset and that means requiring another verification code

Suggestion: get a used more recent Samsung or other top brand if you want to save money

reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 28, 2026

reviews.author: Khaled
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on July 25, 2025
reviews.content:
I’ve been using the OSCAL FLAT 2 Android 15 Phone (2025) for a couple of weeks now, and I’m honestly blown away by how much value it offers at this price point! The performance is smooth and responsive, thanks to the Octa-Core processor and 12GB RAM (4+8 virtual). Multitasking, gaming, and streaming have all been effortless.

The 128GB storage (expandable up to 2TB!) is a huge plus, and I love not worrying about running out of space. The 6.56" HD+ screen with a 90Hz refresh rate makes everything look crisp and fluid—great for watching videos or browsing.

Battery life is excellent with the 5000mAh battery easily lasting a full day, and the Fingerprint + Face ID unlock is fast and secure. I also appreciate the dual SIM capability and solid 4G connectivity on T-network.

If you're looking for a reliable, feature-rich unlocked phone that doesn’t break the bank, this is a fantastic choice. The blue color is sleek too! Highly recommended!

reviews.author: Mike
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on July 29, 2015
reviews.content:
Just some quick first impressions. I really wanted the 2gb Ram/16gb model but it wasn't available and I was hoping to get a feel for the phone early on with some business travel coming up (review is based on the 1gb/8gb model).

Packaging is clean - the device comes in slim packaging compared with previous Nexus 6/Moto E packaging, however is nicely protected. When unboxing and flipping the phone over the first thing I noticed is the instructions on the back panel of the phone on how to properly remove the cover/reseal it in order to get the best water resistance. I also ordered a blue cover from Motorola directly which should arrive next week. While the back cover is removable the battery is not user replaceable - this may be a factor for some but I carry an external battery when travelling for work.

The device itself does not by any means feel like a budget smart phone. It's leaps and bounds better in terms of overall build quality compared to some recently released devices such as the Huawei P8 Lite or Alcatel Idol 3. It is also much more refined than last years G and this years Moto E. The US Model ships with a MicroSD card slot and a single sim card slot (XT1540). I really like the textured rear cover although the device may end up going into a case - I had never dropped cell phones in using them for 10 or so years until recently when I managed to drop them 2 or 3 times over the course of the past month.

The display seems decent enough for a 720p offering, never for a moment did I look at it and feel it was a huge downgrade over something like the Idol 3 or iPhone 6 (PPI will be somewhat lower however it still offers a nice display and most likely a power savings driving a lower res display).

Performance, while it does not seem to lag by any means is not quite up to par with the afformentioend devices however, as the Moto G - Gen 3 ships with a Snapdragon 410 instead of a 615 as many midrange devices now utilize. By no means did I feel performance was poor, but when benchmarking/gaming you will absolutely notice a performance delta between the Moto G and something utilizing a SD6xx/8xx.

Performance actually seems better somehow (perhaps a better Android image or maybe because it ships with 5.1.1?) than the Moto E which I haven't tried with 5.1+ quite yet. I'll update later with experiences once I've had a chance to do so. Antutu turned a benchmark of 23,282 which is fairly respectable for a budget device - for comparison the 2nd generation Moto G using a SnapDragon 400 turned in a score of 17,000 so there is a significant enough performance boost with the new G - roughly a 27% performance improvement over its predecessor.

Battery Life I really couldnt say quite yet as I have it on the charger right now and haven't had much of a chance to utilize the device operating off of its battery quite yet but I will update the review once I have a chance to further utilize. I will say the charge time seemed significant however.

Conslusion:
I'm really happy with the device so far and really don't regret purchasing the 1gb model at this point. I put a 32gb Micro SD card in to provide some additional storage and seemed to be able to move some apps to SD through application manager. I don't think anyone coming from a previous gen model would be dissatisfied with the changes made to the 2015 Moto G. I'll update as I have more of a chance to use the device.

reviews.author: Mangi
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 1, 2016
reviews.content:
Much love & respect for Motorola who've outdone themselves once again by giving us the best budget smartphone money can buy.
Motorola's Moto G series started and so far has been on the forefront of budget smartphone market which has now gotten so better & competitive that they've been dubbed "Flagship killers".
I can start to bog you down with all the techie jargon & technical specifications of this phone but it is unnecessary, leave those to the 5% nerds & geeks. It all bogs down to the overall day to day user experience the 95% typical phone users like me(and probably you) who just wants to get first and foremost, a clean software experience not filled with unnecessary OEM bloatware that I never use or need, nice & comfortable feel on the hand, reliable internet access, access to emails, access to social media(fb, Instagram, Whatsapp, tumblr...) , be able to listen/stream to my favorite radio stations & podcasts, get navigation information, weather updates, SMS(do people still use these??), Voice/VoIP/Video calls, the occasional selfie/group photo/random photo moments, listen/stream music from my playlists or favorite artists on Soundcloud/Spotify.... watch cat videos on youtube ;-) , organize my life and track important dates/events with calendar/notes, casual browsing or shopping on Amazon/ebay/bestbuy, play candy crush etc... Then this phone can do all that without a hiccup. No gimmicks, just pure day to day usability.

And the best feature in my opinion from Motorola with all their devices apart from the very competitive pricing, is the guarantee for timely(maybe not so much after Lenovo took over from Google) software updates. I bought the Moto G 1st gen back in 2013 when it was running Jellybean, I was able to upgrade it through over the air(OTA) to KitKat & handed it down to my little sister who now has upgraded it to Lollipop without a hustle, just purely OTA updates from the good people at Motorola themselves. I have a Moto G 2nd gen from 2014 and this Moto G 3rd gen from 2015 & I believe all(or at least the 2nd & 3rd gen) Moto Gs will get the Marshmallow update(I heard soak tests are already underway in select European and South American Markets) ISN'T THAT AMAZING!!!!!!! Motorola made a promise & they've been able to keep their word, maybe not as smooth & timely as planned but still, they kept their promise and I respect & admire them for that. We usually know that other OEMs only update their flagships(non flagships are left to be forgotten) in a usually messy and painstakingly long wait. Except google's own Nexus line of devices, It is unheard of in the android ecosystem(due to fragmentation) for OEMs to support 3year old devices through OS updates. Much Kudos to Motorola for changing that, other OEMs should follow Motorola's example, there is nothing worse than buying a 600$+ flagship android device(let alone a non flagship) that will be a dinosaur in the next 8months just because the OEM didn't provide an update of the latest Android OS version either because they see no financial value to doing so or they are struggling with the mostly unnecessary customization to the lousy skins they slap on stock android.

VERDICT: If you want a phone that is RELIABLE, has GOOD PERFORMANCE, has a REASONABLE PRICE and will be able to KEEP UP(software updates) with the android ecosystem for the next 2 or 3 years(maybe more...) then look no further, this is the best companion for you. Otherwise if you want a 1000$ phone to match your 2000$ bracelet or if you want a phone to power your hadron particle collider in your secret lab down your basement or if you want a phone to photograph distant stars and solar systems in the sky at night or if you want a phone to record 5hour 4K videos of your cat purring or if you want a phone to play the latest version of GTA/FIFA/Fallout/World of Warcraft/Halo/Assassins Creed/Tomb Raider/... in 4K at 10000FPS or if you want a phone with battery life to last you through the imminent zombie apocalypse... sorry, this is NOT the phone for you, please look elsewhere( I know a friend of mine who is crew in the Enterprise NX-01, he's been to places where they might have what you are looking for. He might be able to help...)

reviews.author: Eric E
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 24, 2016
reviews.content:
Overall I am very happy with this phone. Most of the reviews covered the phone in general so I am not going to discuss the phone itself in depth in this review except for two quick things.

1. One thing that I find annoying about this phone is that it doesnt sit flat on surfaces because of the curved design on the back. The phone will wobble or spin around if you try to use it on a flat surface. Consider getting a case that will allow it to sit flat on surfaces.

2. You should seriously consider the 16gb storage / 2gb RAM model. This is the model I got. On average my phone uses about 1.1GB of memory. I included a screenshot of the memory consumption of my phone. Also, after installing all necessary apps the phone storage space sits at 9.75gb out of 16gb. So you need to decide if the extra $33 is worth it. I included a couple screenshots of the space limitations of this phone.

Camera
I am very pleased with the camera's performance. I got this phone mainly because I didnt want to carry around a camera and a cell phone all the time. I needed to get a new phone but didnt want to pay the price for an LG or iPhone. I find the camera to perform exceptionally well. While there are limitations in the camera app, there are other cool camera apps that could be downloaded that allow for more features.

All the photos attached to this review were taken with the native camera app that came with the phone. The HDR component does an exceptional job in taking outdoor photos.

Flash / Low Light
I took a couple pictures comparing indoor shots with and without a flash. The images are labelled accordingly. I am still not sure about indoor photos and the quality of the flash. In some photos, I felt that the flash sometimes makes the picture look worse. I think this is an overall issue with all smartphones. In very dark situations the quality with a flash was acceptable.

Outdoor
I am very impressed with the quality of the outdoor photos. I really think the photos came out much sharper with the Moto G than with my Panasonic Lumix camera. The camera uses HDR technology which ensures that all elements of the photo come out sharp by taking multiple shots and combining them together for one sharp photo. This means that the image size will much larger when using HDR. This is something else to consider. The non-HDR photos were around 2mb each whereas the HDR images were around 5-6mb each. Included are some really nice landscape and flower shots.

One more thing. The phone recently had a hard fall after I tripped on the stairs (yes I was looking at my phone. I know, I know). This was the second time it fell or perhaps thrown to the hard stone ground. I had the thin clear Pleson case with the Pleson screen protector and the phone didnt have a scratch. The first time it had no case but it did have the Pleson screen protector. It fell scree face down from the table on to a ceramic tiled floor and nothing happened to the screen. Dont waste your money on a bulky otterbox style case. Not needed.

reviews.author: Ryan M
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on October 19, 2015
reviews.content:
This is actually a great phone. For the price, I was not expecting much, but after using it for the past couple weeks I am very satisfied. My previous phone was an Iphone 5C, and I accidentally dropped my phone and cracked the screen. I'm with At&t, and did NOT want to do their new plan with At&t Next. To get a new Iphone was something like $27/month for 30 months which is $800. Their other phones are around the same price, and I really do not want to pay that much for a phone. I really just use it to text, make calls, and check traffic. I was also looking at a Lumia 640, but I am ultimately glad I went with this phone. I like both phones, but the lack of apps on windows phones is a killer. I don't use many apps, but the apps I use, I use frequently and aren't on windows phones.

Compared with my Iphone 5c, I actually find this to be a better phone! I've read reviews saying you NEED to get the 16 GB 2 GB ram version of this phone, but I can tell you it's not very necessary unless you really use your phone alot and maybe do alot of gaming. I got the 1 GB RAM, and it is more than enough for me. The onboard 8 GB memory is not much, but I just bought a $10 microSD card and bumped it up to 32 GB. The old Iphone 5 actually has the same 1 GB RAM, and this hasn't lagged at all even when i've had alot of applications open.

It took me a little while to get used to coming from an Iphone for the past 5 years or so, but I am glad I made the switch. The camera is great, and I like how it's connected to the Google line of products. I use Gmail and Chrome, and works seamlessly with their apps and I imported all my contacts and information from those accounts to the phone. I started getting irritated with Iphones when they made that switch in the connectors. I had alot of stereos and stuff that had those old connectors, then they switched to the lightning usb. Also with Iphones, whenever theres a new Iphone and you update to the new IOS on your current one it happens to "mysteriously" slow down to a crawl and is not very usable. Pretty sure they do this on purpose to get you to buy a new Iphone. Anyway, I got tired of Iphones and am ultimately satisfied with this android phone. It does everything I need to, and is pretty user friendly. I just took to my local At&t store, and they put in a new sim card for no charge. Have had no problems with service and calls are clear. Battery life is great

reviews.author: FixYourJunk
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on July 9, 2017
reviews.content:
The last Moto G from from the Motorola team. In many ways Lenovo isn't doing a horrible job with the G4 and G5. But they are simply a bit of a downgrade for the G line. First of all, the water resistance IPX7-rating was dropped from all NEW moto phones with the G3 being the lowest cost phone on the market to have such a rating. The very pure Android UI is welcomed and still that way with the new G models.

The 5" screen is really about as large as you can get before it becomes too large for the hand and pockets. Even the 5.5+ bezel-less phones are larger overall.

What is regrettably lost on all new Moto phones is the body shape. They could have made the old moto-style shell for the Z model or make a modernized C model with the similar shape and size. The shape of the old X and G models fit the hand very well, you can feel if its upside down and they are not super-thin which makes them harder to pick up.

The new Moto E (2017 -3rd gen) has slightly newer and better tech for $130, that is otherwise slightly better than the two-year old G3 which typically goes for $160~250 today. But I bought this to replace my original G3 (I paid $230) which I even used for under-water video. After several drops at almost 2 years - the screen landed on a large bolt and shattered past the glass but otherwise worked. I still had 5 colored shells, the replacement gives me 6 choices now. Something the E can't do, besides I think having a camera bump on the E3 and G5 is problematic and open to scratches.

I didn't see the point in spending $180 for the G4 that offered nothing or the G5 at $230 that has an even bigger screen that I don't want. I'd prefer the AMOLED screen of the 5G, higher res and it's much better camera. But I want my next purchase of a modern phone to include the USB-C
connector. Maybe the 6G will have what I want in a $200~230 phone. Not counting on water-resistance and I DON'T buy $500~800 phones that I would still end up replacing in 2 years when the battery gets weak, the screen not as smooth, the body banged up. (I don't see the point of spending $700 for a beautiful glass phone that you have to buy a $30 case to cover it up to help protect it from shattering- yeah, I'm talking about you Samsung)

The Moto G3 - a very good OLD phone... when you need just the basics. A phone, GPS and browsing ability. If you need to play games and do photos, then look at the Moto G5 or maybe some other phones from Blu, Huawei, ASUS etc in the $130~200 price range.

If you can get a used G3 for $80~130, its a bargain. Make sure you get the 16GB version as it has 2GB of system memory compared to the 8GB version with 1GB. This effects the phone's performance especially if you have several apps open.

The Moto G3 gets a 5 star rating today for what you get for the price. The G4 & G5 are not rated as high for a few reasons which make them a disappointment to customers like me. So get them while you can. Eventually only worn out used models will be available and we'll have to hope someone else or the Moto G6 or G7 would be a successful replacement of the G3.

reviews.author: Amazon Customer
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on May 22, 2016
reviews.content:
For the price , I suppose this is a pretty decent phone. The screen resolution is nothing to write home about but then again, the dimensions aren't overtly trying to be a phablet. The camera is decent for taking your average shots / selfies etc as well. The phone can get just a tad toasty but then again, what phone doesn't when multitasking. It's nothing to be concerned of however like the Sony Xperia. Battery life as well is pretty average. Several other reviews boast amazing charge times as well as the ability to hold a charge tremendously longer than any iPhone or samsung, but again, it all comes down to what type of apps you're running. Chances are, if you're considering buying this phone, you're not going to be doing heavy gaming or running CPU intensive programs. A final take-away, if you need an android phone, don't want to pay mid tier prices, value quality over looks and functionality, then definitely this is the best phone out there ATM. And hey, if you're the type of user that might jump in the shower, and realize you forgot to text your boss telling him you're running late, no need to wipe off your hands...haha!

3/5 stars, only because it's really an average phone, that live up to everything it claims, which happens to be... being average.

reviews.author: Quixbright
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on August 19, 2015
reviews.content:
Moto G 3rd gen review:

I considered this purchase somewhat carefully and have a lot to say about it. I've only had the phone for a week, but I'm quite satisfied so far. It does all the things I need it to do, plus a few extras thrown in.

What I was looking for:

Price: $200 or less
Size: Must fit in hand
OS: Anything but Apple (price requirement probably excludes that anyway)
Features: WiFi and LTE capability, FM radio, average camera, removeable but decent battery, headphone jack, decent memory/processing and enough storage for some books, music and podcasts.

What I got:
All of those except the removeable battery, plus water resistance and a relatively crisp screen. I did have to buy an SD card to expand the storage from 8GB to 32BG, but I didn't mind that. I don't use a lot of apps, but if you do, you'll probably need more than 8GB.

I also like the quick access to the flashlight and camera. The first time I used the flashlight and camera, a pop-up informed me that I could access either with a chopping motion (flashlight) or twisty motion (camera) even when the phone is locked. I don't know how common that is on smartphones because this is my first one, but the little tutorial tips were easy to follow. The only drawback to this is that someone could take pictures with your locked phone. In order to view the pictures already on the phone, however, it has to be unlocked. This doesn't bother me personally, but could be an issue for some folks. I believe this feature can be disabled.

What I use it for:
Texting, getting email alerts so I don't have to be logged into my work and personal email AND near my computer to know I have an email, calendar reminders and general organization, alarm clock, Kindle replacement (I just can't read on my desktop), amateurish snapshots, Netflix, web browsing, viewing and editing work documents on Google (just quick, little things, not extended work), maps, flashlight.

What I do not use/need:
1) LTE. I don't have a data plan and don't intend to get one any time soon. I wanted the capability so I'd have that option, since I don't plan on buying a new phone until I absolutely HAVE to. I can't comment on how well the phone works for on-the-go data b/c I use it exclusively with wifi.

2) Camera. I mean, I use the camera, but I'm not any sort of judge of the quality. I've had trouble with blurry pictures, but I'm 99% certain that's user error. When I take my time, the pictures look good to me. Better than what I really need, in fact.

3) Navigation. Since I don't have a data plan, I only use it as a replacement for a physical map. I download maps ahead of time if I think I'll need them, but otherwise I don't use this function. I suspect the quality of the GPS would have more to do with the network than the phone anyhow.

4) Lots of processing power. I'm not a "power user." I have a desktop for that. When I'm out and about, I'm doing something else, so I don't have a lot of need for a lightening fast, top-of-the-line model. However, I have to say that for the price, this phone moves pretty fast. There's very little lag in the interface and most web pages and videos load without issue on wifi. The Imgur app runs pretty smoothly, even when viewing large albums. So does Google maps. For me, the phone goes above and beyond in that regard.

Other models I looked at:
BLU Studio Energy
Moto E
Samsung Galaxy Core Prime

Why I chose this model over those:
The Studio Energy's superpowered battery appealed to me, but when I tried one out at the store, it moved rather sluggishly. There were also some reports online of stability issues and defects. The volume and quality of some of those reports gave me pause.

The Samsung model seemed good on paper, but some reviews mentioned that the display and camera were subpar for the price. The price difference between the Moto E and Moto G was small considering the extra power the G has. And I liked the way the display on the G looked: very crisp and pretty visible in full sunlight if you turn the brightness all the way up. All in all, the Moto G just seemed like a better phone for the price. And everyone I know who has had a Motorola phone (including me) liked it and experienced few problems.

I'm sure there are other phones out there that would have served my needs, but so far this one is working out splendidly. It definitely doesn't feel like a budget phone. The only cons are the battery life and the fact that the battery is not removeable. Not that the battery drains THAT quickly; even with a few hours of listening to the radio and streaming video, it still lasts the whole day. But if you're going to use it a LOT, you'll need a battery pack or something. The fact that I can't remove/replace the battery bothers me, but again, the tradeoff is the water resistance. My previous (flip) phones sustained water damage just from being in my pocket when it was hot out (EVERYBODY SWEATS, OKAY?) or rained. According to one review I watched, the phone kept playing video when submerged in a glass of water. Though it should be noted that it is only water RESISTANT. Motorola doesn't claim that you can submerge it in liquid without damage. However, I do like that little bit of extra protection, since I want to be able to keep the phone for a while.

If I run into problems, I will update, but so far I'm very happy with this purchase. I feel like I got a lot (though obviously not all) of the benefits of joining everyone in the 21st century without paying the horrendous price tag that many people pay. ($800 for a phone? You could put together a reasonably decent gaming desktop for that. It's just not my bag.)

THREE-YEAR UPDATE: I dropped this phone a million times (went through three or four of those ten-dollar cases). The flashlight broke (loose connection - light only comes on if you squeeze the phone) and the screen became separated from the case. Screen cracked when I tried to pop it back. I think all those falls damaged the hardware because there were performance issues near the end. Given the level of punishment I subjected it to, I'm fully satisfied with the three years it gave me. Shame the new Motorola budget models don't have the water resistance, though.

reviews.author: Piaw Na
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on November 16, 2015
reviews.content:
Never plug your phone into an untested charger that's not made by the OEM. I learned the hard way when I plugged my phone into a newly bought charger (my wife bought it in an effort to get rid of the rat's nest of wires on the charging desk --- needless to say it's the most expensive charger she's ever bought --- lesson: keep your rats nest and stick with well-reviewed chargers). Well, my Xperia Z1's motherboard got fried, and I had to shop for a new phone in a hurry.

I liked the Xperia Z1, so my initial thought was to just simply purchase an Xperia Z3. Alas, the price I got from Amazon did turn out to be too good to be true. Upon receiving the phone, I checked it against Sony's website and discovered that the "new" phone I was sold only had 2 months of warranty left. In other words, some reseller had bought the phone from Sony or T-mobile, and was reselling it to me as "new." The full price of the phone ($550) was more than I could stomach, so I went looking for other choices.

My criteria were:
Waterproof: having learned from the Xperia Z1 how useful a feature that was, I wasn't willing to give this up.
MicroSD card storage. Sorry, I'm not paying $100 to get an extra 16GB of storage. That's for Apple users. (I paid $25 for a 64GB MicroSD card)
2GB of RAM, preferably 3.
5" screen. More than that, and it's too awkward to hold one-handed.
Camera shutter button preferred.
Cheap. Let's face it, phone processor performance hasn't improved in years
Not surprisingly, other than the Xperia Z series, there are only a few phones that offer the first feature:
Sony M4 Aqua ($250)
Motorola Moto G ($220)
Samsung Galaxy S5 ($400)
The Samsung was too much to pay for, and I never liked the UI, so it came down to the Sony Aqua and the Moto G. I honestly did not expect the Moto G to be a contender, but since Lenovo acquired them from Google, Motorola has finally put microSD card storage even into its high end phones like the Moto X. Back when Google owned Motorola, it suffered from severe Apple envy and thought it could charge Apple-type prices for storage. The addition of waterproofing was a nice surprise. In fact, the higher-end phones in Motorola's line up all only have water-resistance, rather than being IP7 waterproof. I would have considered the shatter-proof Droid Turbo 2 if it had included water-proofing along with the 4 year anti-crack warranty. As it was, I figured I could get 3 Moto Gs for the price of one of those, and waterproofing trumps crack-proofing.

My experience with the Sony Z1 was that I was constantly getting applications killed. I'd play music and turn on navigation and music would die. I've also had the reverse happen. That made me wary of getting another Sony phone with just 2GB of RAM. (For whatever reason, this doesn't happen to my wife's Xperia Ultra Z, which at 6.5" in size is so big that it also has wonderful voice and data reception!)

Reviews consistently state that the Moto G has a better camera (surprising, since the Moto G's camera is a Sony sensor). The Sony M4 Aqua also has a faster processor, NFC, supports up to 128GB of microSD storage and is thinner. Both phones are 720p 5" displays, which is fine. What's interesting is that the Moto G did support my 64GB SD card, despite not being officially in the specs. I did have to reformat it with the phone, but after that it worked just great. It's unusual for companies to under promise and over-deliver, so I wonder what's going on there.

In practice, however, the Moto G actually appears faster than the M4 Aqua, even in the 1GB configuration! In the end, between the camera and improved performance, I went with the Moto G. Later research showed that the Snapdragon 615 used in the M4 Aqua has severe throttling issues due to overheating, so I unwittingly dodged a bullet.

Since I was buying in a hurry to replace a dead phone, I ordered the black version from Amazon rather than going through Moto Maker. It's also much easier to return something to Amazon than to Motorola, so that was a consideration.

The phone itself is fast, as fast as the Xperia Z1. Installing software, starting up software all felt faster than the Z1 (undoubtedly helped by not having to push as many pixels), while task switching was surprisingly quick with no latency experienced. The camera was about as fast as the Z1's, though obviously picture quality isn't anywhere close to the benchmark set by that camera. The twist to shoot gesture is a nice gimmick, and I thought it to be useless but to my surprise after a few days with the phone I could shoot with the camera while cycling! Video is surprisingly good:

By far the biggest issue with the phone is sluggish bluetooth pairing. In fact, for both my cars and the SBH52, I had to reboot the phone in order to do a pairing. Neither the Logitech X100 nor my vivoactive (which unfortunately needed a factory reset to unstick its pairing from the Z1) needed such a reboot. Once paired, the pairing sticks, so at least this is only a one time problem.

Sound quality is, not surprisingly, lacking compared to the Z1, but not so much so that I found it objectionable. Some have reported annoying cross-talk when plugging in headphones into the microphone jack. I was prepared to have to do without (most of my listening is via blue-tooth over the SBH52 anyway), but when I plugged in headphones it sounded just fine.

To my surprise, going from the Z1's 1080p screen down to the 720p screen didn't bother me at all. Of course, watching movies on the Z1 has always drained the battery so fast that I rarely did it, and in any case to save storage I'd always watched movies in 720p.

Motorola is well known for providing a near-stock Android experience with no additional UI tweaks. I didn't expect this to make a big difference to me, but it does and is a pleasant welcome after the Sony modifications to the OS. I also expect that this also contributed largely to the higher performance of the UI and software despite the supposedly slower processor.

After my experience with the Z1, which begged to be recharged nearly all the time, so much so that I put the phone into stamina mode full time, any change had to be better. The Moto G was disappointing at first, barely lasting 12 hours without a charge. But 2 charge cycles later the battery life improved dramatically, with me typically ending the day somewhere around 40-50% of battery life. On a heavy use day it'd drop to 15%. This is a huge improvement over the Xperia Z1, and makes use of say, the Garmin Livetrack feature much more feasible than before. With the Xperia Z1, even with stamina mode, I was unlikely to make it to the end of the day without a mid-day recharge. Lithium ion batteries will generally lose 20% of their capacity after 300 recharge cycles. If your battery is barely able to get you through the day, after a year, your battery will absolutely not get you through the day. And yes, this applies even if you're the type to keep it plugged in as often as possible. The relatively long battery life of the Moto G means that you can expect to get at least 2 years of use out of the phone before the non-replaceable battery starts to lose enough charge capacity to be annoying.

All in all, given the price of the phone and the features (waterproofing is huge for me), the performance of the phone is such that I will be happy to hang on to it for a good long time. I expect that if you manage to buy this phone during the inevitable black friday sales, you'll get it for a significant discount which will be an even better deal.

Recommended.

reviews.author: william f clements
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 19, 2026
reviews.content: was a wonderful phone, until I lost it, not kidding

reviews.author: Regillio Padma
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on October 17, 2023
reviews.content:
If you are used to higher end budget phones, this is not a phone for you.
If you are like me, always on a tight budget, then this may work for you.
Depending on your needs, you may have issues with the quality of the pictures, sometimes the fingerprint reader doesn't read, face recognition works half the time, build feels cheap.
But for me it's perfect! I'm going from a Motorola e6 to this and the difference is HUGE!
It is a bit big (duuh).
At the moment I have DLS23, FC Mobile, Lightning Link games and other banking apps etc. installed and it handles it very well. No lagging.
I have the phone for 3 weeks and have no issues.
Calls are clear, the speaker is ok, it has more low end and sounds clearer compared to the other phone.
What I like the most is the DOLBY ATMOS app! Music sounds zoo much better with this on!
Battey life, I don't know about the 2 day, cause if you play games a lot it will drain it.
For now, I'm charging it once daily and I play games about 2-4 hours a day on it.
So for my needs, it is a good phone overall!
UPDATE 18-JULY-2025:
AFTER ALMOST 2 YEARS FACE RECOGNIZTION NOT ALWAYS WORKING, BATTERY BEGINNING TO DRAIN FASTER WHEN PLAYING GAMES, BUT IF YOUR USING FOR CALLS, TEXTING AND DOOM SCROLLING CAN LAST A WORKING DAY. DOLBY ATMOS NOT ALWAYS WORKING. FELL ALOT WITH A CRACKED SCREEN. STILL WORKING.
PHONE STILL FUNCTIONS.

reviews.author: Nighat Ashfaq
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on September 20, 2024
reviews.content:
I've been using the Motorola Moto G Stylus (2022) for about a year now, and overall, it’s a solid phone, especially for the $140 price point. I see that they have increased the price now. It’s not flashy by any means, but for everyday use, it gets the job done.

The battery life is the best I've got to say. It's the main reason I bought it. The 2-day battery holds up, and I don't often find myself connecting it to a charger. Even with moderate usage, like whether you are scrolling, texting, or doing some light gaming, it lasts the entire day and beyond. 

The 50MP camera is decent. In good lighting, photos come out satisfactory, but it struggles a bit in low light. I personally edit the photos later using the adjusting tool in the camera app; it makes it reasonably sharp and clear. The camera app is easy to use. 

The stylus is also a cool feature that I didn’t think I’d use much, but it’s actually handy for quick notes and drawing.

Let's talk performance. It’s good for the price. It handles most tasks without too much lag. There are occasional slowdowns when switching between apps simultaneously when you are on a call; it becomes annoying, but it's not much of a deal breaker.

The phone’s build is nice and big, and the color looks great. It feels comfortable in the hand, although it's a bit on the larger and heavier side. It becomes a little tricky when I don't have pants with bigger pockets. So keep that in mind if you prefer more compact phones.

Overall, if you’re looking for a budget friendly phone that has reasonable performance, a good battery, and a few extra features like the stylus, the Moto G Stylus is a good choice. For $140, it’s hard to beat in this segment, but don’t expect it to perform like a high end phone. It’s good value for what you pay!

reviews.author: Kathy
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on November 18, 2024
reviews.content:
I really do love this phone.
I had a different android phone and it basically sucked. The reception was terrible, screen froze constantly.

This Moto phone is crystal clear on calls, battery life is great and the speed for any apps is amazing! I love that I waited to get the model with the built in stylus. There are so many wonderful features to learn. The camera is awesome :) lots of features.
If you're on the fence on this one, jump off it's worth it. My brother owes a Apple phone which is nice but for the cost compared to this, save $$$$ take a chance with this phone. Definitely worth it.

reviews.author: Pedro Espinal
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on August 9, 2025
reviews.content: I’m very happy with this purchase. The item arrived on time and was exactly as described in the listing. The quality is excellent, and it works just as expected. It feels well-made and reliable, and I’ve had no issues with it so far. Overall, a great value for the price. Would definitely recommend this seller and product!

reviews.author: Adrian Reyes
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 4, 2026
reviews.content: I’m impressed the battery life easily lasts all day and the display in incredibly vibrant, highly recommended

reviews.author: Alex
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on May 23, 2024
reviews.content:
TL;DR: Doesn't maintain connection to WiFi, actual "phone" function would randomly stop working, would freeze during start up, failed to correctly boot and needed a factory reset, not at all water resistant, stylus is very basic and not pressure sensitive, very heavy and bulky for a phone. Battery lasted well, speaker and camera were decent, and had some nice software features.

The phone was great, for the first month, until I started to notice particular issues with it. It doesn't maintain a WiFi connection. It'll say that it's connected, but then pages won't load and it'll endlessly be buffering until I turned the WiFi off and suddenly it was a fast as lightning. I own numerous devices that connected to the same WiFi with absolutely no issues, and I even tried using different WiFi hotspots, but always had the same issue. I even tried configuring the phone settings to see if that would help, but it never did.

What really bothered me about this phone though, was the fact that after a certain period of time after it was on (I'm not sure exactly how long, but definitely less than 24 hours) suddenly the actual PHONE part of this "smartphone" would stop working. It would still be able to use data, and I was able to send /receive messages via social media, but I wouldn't be able to make/receive phone calls or texts. I'd suddenly get a voicemail, while using my phone, stating that I missed a call when it didn't ring. And if I tried to make a call, the call would immediately end without ringing. The only way I figured out to resolve this (even after searching Google for more ways to configure the settings), was to restart the phone.

Which brings me to my third issue. A small percentage of the time, it would restart in a normal, timely manner (1-2 minutes), but most of the time it would freeze on the startup screen for at least 5 minutes before finally loading in. Sometimes, if I held the power button and manually turned it off and back on, it would fix it, but not usually.

My fourth issue, was that sometimes if I plugged it into the charger, it would suddenly restart. It didn't seem like too much of a big deal at the time, until one of the times that it happened, it froze ENTIRELY on the startup screen. I tried doing what I did previously to hopefully get it to start again, but it continued to freeze. After searching online for answers and trying to troubleshoot it, the only answer I was able to find... Was to manually factory reset it. I would have considered bringing it it somewhere to be looked at by a professional, but I didn't have the time or the money for that, especially since I was not at home and needed the phone ASAP. Doing this was an absolute last resort for me and I was devastated.

Finally, what brought about the death of this phone, was that it's definitely not waterproof. I'll admit that this was entirely my fault, but I'd like to mention this as a warning to those who are used to nearly indestructible water resistant phones (like the Samsung Galaxy series). It happened to be on the sink counter beside me while I was brushing my teeth and I accidentally knocked it into the water stream. The charging port ended up frying and it no longer charges. Being that I had so many issues with it though, I'm thankful to have a reason to move on.

Also, I'd like to mention that if you're used to responsive pressure sensitive styluses (like the Galaxy Note series), the stylus for this phone is nothing like that. It's exactly like a stylus you'd buy from the dollar store, except it looks nice and fits inside the phone.

Another thing I'd like to note, is that this phone is very heavy compared to other phones and kind of bulky. I like to use PopSockets, and I never had issues with them before, but while using this phone I had to replace the base about once a month because it would just start peeling off, due to the weight. I ended up having to super glue it to my phone case, which resolved the issue. It also hurt my hand quite a bit while holding it for any length of time.

Despite all of these things, it did have numerous features that I liked, but they were software related, so I imagine they would probably be available on most Motorola phones. The battery also lasted a decent amount of time as well, about a day and a half as long as I didn't do too much gaming. The speaker was also nice and loud, but a little tinny at times. The camera was decent too, and I was able to take some nice pictures with it.

The cons definitely outweigh any positives in my opinion. What the point of a smart phone if it doesn't perform as a phone?

reviews.author: RhythmJunky
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on July 17, 2024
reviews.content:
I wanted to come back and update. I did manage to contact the manufacturer about a warrantee claim. The process was relatively simple and smooth. Evidently there was a problem in the software related to how the IMEI number registers with the cell towers. Now I have it back and I noticed something that wasn't very obvious before. I got a different phone and the display of the new phone is quite good. When I put this phone next to it I could tell the display of the moto g is adequate, but not as bright or vivid. Seeing what else is out there in the same price range, I think it's safe to say this phone is a solid 3 stars.

This particular model has some reliability issues. It's not the network or the sim card. The phone disconnects from the cellular network and you don't know it on home wifi. You miss calls and can't call or text unless you restart the phone. I have the 2021 version and it has worked great. I used it so much I have to replace the battery. The 2022 version is not worth the aggravation. By the way. I don't know if I've ever written a 1 star review for anything. My phone is still within the warranty period but I can't seem to find a way to contact Motorola for help. If I could actually contact them my rating might have looked different. As it is we may never know.

reviews.author: jeff
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on November 18, 2025
reviews.content: Not only does it make calls and send texts this phone really does everything that a modern phone does. Social media apps, google maps and Spotify are all here. Even Android auto works on it. Its really only limited by the small screen.

reviews.author: active reviewer
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 1, 2026

reviews.author: R. Robinette
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on June 7, 2024
reviews.content: Nice little phone. It looks like a modern, quality phone just much smaller. It comes with a nice, clear silicone case that does a good job protecting the phone edges. I took a Mint Mobile SIM out of an Android tablet and put it in the tiny phone and it just worked. I didn't have to do anything with Mint Mobile. Mint uses T-Mobile towers so this phone will work for T-Mobile too. The phone works as expected for phone, text and internet data. No muss, no fuss. This phone is definitely 4g as I get "LTE" on the signal indicator. This phone is an amazing value at this price point.

reviews.author: Jen
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 18, 2026
reviews.content: This is the perfect little starter phone. We’ve recently started leaving our almost ten year old at home for short stretches (30 mins at a time or less), and I like him to have a way to call us besides using the Alexa. This is perfect! It’s small but otherwise functions just like a normal phone. We set it up with a SIM card from T-Mobile, and it works great. It’s a little slow processing, but works perfect for what we got it for.

It's a really cute phone! I very much wanted it to work out, but it didn't fit our needs. For a kid it would probably be fine.

I wanted something for my mother, who finds most smart phones too heavy and hard to fit in a pocket. This seemed like just the thing. Unfortunately, the on-screen keyboard and font are too tiny. A finger or even a stylus covers multiple letters, making it very finicky to type. 🫤 A kid might have smaller fingers and better eye sight. Oh, it was also rather slow... It spun the hourglass on sending a one line text. Pity! It is a really light, cute form factor.

reviews.author: AK Dubey
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 3, 2025
reviews.content:
I got this phone for my daughter, its very cute and see loves it. It can handle basic functions like call, text and socail media
Here are some pros cons which we felt

Pros:
The 3.0-inch size makes it extremely lightweight and easy to carry in pockets or small bags. so great for kids or students or as secondary phone
Supports 4G networks,I did not have issues with cell phone Signals.
Its unlocked so can be used with majority of providers , we tested both with AT&T and Visible (Verizon) without any issues
I has dual sim , so if needed can have 2 lines same time though i did not verify this feature
It has decent Storage for basic use: With 2GB RAM and 16GB storage, it can handle light applications like calling, texting, and social media.
Its priced lower than mainstream smartphones, making it an affordable alternative.

Cons:
The 3.0-inch display might be too tiny for comfortable typing, watching videos, or extended browsing.
Camera is just fine and its like high end device and we know premium phones are heavy on pocket so I am ok with the camera.
Since its small phone so battery is proportional and battery lasts 1 full day with basic usages.
As its obvious this is not a phone for high end gaming

Overall, we are happy with it as it works for our need and its light weight on pocket

reviews.author: Bri
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on June 30, 2024
reviews.content: I got this for my 5 YO, I saw the TikTok trend and was like yesss that’s perfect !!! Got it turned on yesterday at the AT&T store with no problems. He put his SIM card in it first just to make sure it works and it did. Then he did something with the IME or whatever. At one point it did say not supported and he unsuspended it. So for those saying it’s not carrier supported… it’s because they don’t know what they’re doing. It 100% does work he also did tell me it will not work for a long time because of the not supported thing that came up and he told me I can come back in and he’ll unsuspend it, but eventually we’ll need to get a new phone. So for now… it does what it needs to. So for Att it does work, and hopefully you can get a rep that actually knows what they’re doing. When using, the battery does drain pretty fast but it’s not horrible. Obviously given the size it’s very hard to text on it but it’s great for little kids. Also it came with a clear case and screen protector which is great because I couldn’t find any on here. If you’re looking for a first time little phone for a small kid just to make calls really, then this would be the perfect start up phone !

reviews.author: M
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 8, 2026
reviews.content: I almost never write reviews, but I can already tell this is going to make such a difference in my life! reviews.author: Michael Porter
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on September 18, 2017
reviews.content:
This is one awesome phone. The worst thing I can say about it is that since it's all glass, you NEED some kind of skin around it; it's the slipperiest phone ever, I think. I'm a big BB fan, and while the Passport is still my all-time favorite phone, I have to say that the DTEK 60 is simply an amazing piece of technology. It's fast - blazing fast. Apps open without any issue, from graphics-intense games to HD videos. The screen is amazing and smudge-free! The cameras are by far the best ever on any BB and compare favorably to any camera on any Samsung or iPhone. I don't like any virtual keyboard, but the VKB on this (as well on the PRIV and DTEX 50) is really nice and intuitive. And the fingerprint sensor is fast and accurate.

Bottom line is that this is a high end phone. It may not be a flagship phone, but for $500 dollars, you're getting a really good phone/camera/media center. It's well-worth the price. I also own a KEYone and while the K1 is a rock solid phone with an awesome physical keyboard, it seems to me that the DTEK 60 is a better phone. I hate to say it. If you're looking for a new phone and don't want to lay out grand for a new iPhone, give the DTEK 60 a spin. You won't be disappointed in the least.

reviews.author: Monica Mitchell
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on April 1, 2025
reviews.content:
I purchased the LIVELY Jitterbug Smart4 for my 93-year-old mom, and it’s been a fantastic choice overall. She’s never been very tech-savvy, but this phone is designed with clarity and simplicity in mind. The large screen, big text, and straightforward menu make it easy for her to use without getting overwhelmed.

The speaker is loud and clear, which is a huge plus for anyone with hearing difficulties. She can hear it ring from another room, and phone calls are crisp and easy to follow. The touch screen is responsive, and the simplified home screen keeps everything she needs—like the phone, camera, and messages—right up front.

Setup was relatively easy, and customer support through Lively has been very friendly and patient. Just note that the phone must be activated with a Lively plan—it’s not compatible with other carriers, so make sure that works for your needs before purchasing.

The only slight downside is the learning curve that comes with any smartphone for someone her age—but with time and encouragement, she’s gotten the hang of calling, texting, and even using the camera.

✅ Excellent for seniors with vision or hearing concerns
✅ Large icons, simple interface, and great support
✅ Peace of mind with emergency features if needed

I feel good knowing she’s connected and safe with a phone that was truly made for her stage in life. Would definitely recommend to others caring for elderly parents.

reviews.author: War
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 1, 2026
reviews.content:
I work in a local cell phone store, where I see people daily purchase the standard smart phone only to come back in the next day frustrated because the phone is to advanced for them. The standard cell phone is not user friendly for an elder
person, however the Jitterbug Smart4 is.

I purchased the Smart4 through Amazon as gift for my elder mother. I had the phone up and online within 30mins.
You have the option of transferring your present phone number or you can get a new phone number through Lively. Payments, trouble-shooting, support are all through Lively which is an independent network.

reviews.author: Kamazonrevs
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 29, 2025
reviews.content: Bought this for an elderly family member. works well n nearest to a smartphone that works well for seniors. downside is the volume doesn't go high enough, even at 100% setting it's really not that loud

reviews.author: Amazon Customer
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on June 20, 2025
reviews.content:
I purchased the Jitterbug phone for my sister and unfortunately it did not work out. The reason why I returned it was there is no speed dialing. To make a call it required 5 steps. And It was not possible to add a contact to the menu.

I called to cancel the cell plan and they refused to refund the $68 or prorate it. I only had the phone for about a week and spent all that time trying to get the phone to work. You read all about how you have 30 days to try it out. I thought if it didn't work out I would not be charged for the monthly phone plan or it would be prorated. I feel this is misleading. And I have been taken advatage of.

There are a lot of other problems.

Unlimited Data
Unlimited data plan slows down after 20 gb. From 300 mg to 128 kb. It crawls. And if you think 20 gb is a lot it is not. My sister is in a nursing home, their wifi is always down and she uses between 35 - 40 gb a month. Just playing games. No video, no music just games.

Setup:
It took more than 2 hours to setup the phone. Activation took 40 mins. They would not contact me directly to an agent to help me set up the phone. I had to wait 15 mins while they reset the phone. I had to call back 3 times each time I had to wait 15 mins then call back. I was also disconnected once. After an hour I finally reached an agent who was great, Diane, she remotely accessed my phone and did a great job. the call lasted 68 mins.

Factory Resets:
I had to do 2 factory restarts. This is when the phone is comepletly wiped. And you have to start all over again. One time it was because of an update. One time my sister got to a blank screen, I don't know how she did it, and the home key would not work. Restart did not help. I had to do a factory reset.

The phone is 4g not 5g. You might be surprised, but this does make a difference.

I don't write reveiws, positive or negative but I am so upset. I write this reveiw to help you in your research find a good phone for your love one.

reviews.author: Ed Gaillard
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 22, 2026
reviews.content: I bought this for my 85-year-old mother. The keys are large enough for her to see and The screen is nice and bright. I had no problem setting this up for her nor teaching her how to use it. It's a great phone for an elderly person.

reviews.author: Poohbear
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 14, 2026
reviews.content: Great display, easy to use. It is a little slow but fine for my elder parent to use. Though the speaker phone even at max volume does still sound a bit low for my hearing impaired parents. If you have hearing aids it does have options for this. Unfortunately my parents do not so this option is of no use to me personally.

reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 10, 2026

I prefer a 'louder' sound, so I purchased an external speaker to hook up, which arrives tomorrow, January 11. I charge it throughout the day in order to keep it at high capacity. I do not miss my old landline, which is affected by heavy rains, which occurs here in Honolulu. And the monthly charge is much less than the landline, saving me money, which I do not mind.

reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 30, 2026
reviews.content: This phone was purchased for someone who ended up using another "dumb" phone instead because this one is definitely bricklike. We did not try it on our network but the features all worked once the battery was charged. The buttons are large, easy to see and easy to press. The cost was great for a "dumb" phone with calling and texting abilities. One star was removed because the back was difficult to remove and required a small lever (we used a tiny screwdriver) to pop it open. It is definitely sturdy but maybe too sturdy for general use.

reviews.author: john bauchrowitz
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on August 4, 2025
reviews.content:
I had the M7, but with such a tiny screen no android is now preferred.
It has an amazing recording microphone and speaker. I actually use it to record
very nice quality audio for Youtube guitar videos. Has nice light, and the speaker sounds better, louder,

reviews.author: Great Dane
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on September 1, 2025

reviews.author: TSelf
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on September 26, 2025
reviews.content:
The speaker is broken, so I cannot play anything or use speaker phone.
-It took a while for T-mobile to even understand what I was talking about when I went in the store to get the right sized SIM card. Now, I do not have an alternate phone to go back to.
-It is also bulkier than I expected.
-The texting features are odd. It would be nice to have a setting that allows for common words to automatically pop up.
-The flashlight is amazingly bright and easy to turn on and off. However, that also means it comes on easily if pressure is applied to the button while in your purse.
-When texting and calling, contact names do not come up to show who is calling/texting.

reviews.author: yolanda
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on April 14, 2025
reviews.content: Absolutely love it. Highly recommend. If.tou have a micro sim make sure you get the sim adapter

reviews.author: Vile Lizard
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on April 19, 2025
reviews.content:
This is clearly a product designed for a specific market, and I'm not quite sure what that market is.

It's solidly built and also pretty water-resistant, with a removable battery and SIM/SD slots inside a double-sealed compartment that's actually fairly difficult to get open. The robust construction and smallish, simple screen should be good for preventing damage in an outdoor or work environment.

As a voice-calling phone, it works great, with dual-SIM support (if you need that on a "dumb" phone) and robust 4G cellular connection. It gets better signal bars and fewer dropouts than my more-modern smart phone, likely because it's huge and has plenty of room for a big antenna.

Anything other than voice, less so. The camera is fairly awful, essentially VGA resolution, and would be right at home on a 90s Nokia. Texting is clumsy, with the interface using a ridiculously large font and silly input strategy with some weird variant of the "T9" predictive typing thing that's often wrong with its predictions and doesn't like to be corrected. I didn't find an obvious way to shrink the font or switch to multi-press typing.

There's an FM radio and a couple little "productivity" apps, which aren't super useful without being able to connect them to anything else.

For a construction worker who appreciates a phone built like a tank, or for a senior who won't ever do anything besides basic phone calls, this might be a good option.

reviews.author: Stephen Gates
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 30, 2024
reviews.format: Style: iPhone 16 Pro MaxColor: Noir Ash Black
reviews.content:
The OtterBox iPhone 16 Pro Max Symmetry Series Cactus Leather case is amazing! The quality and durability are exactly what you’d expect from OtterBox—top-notch. The case feels incredibly sturdy and offers great protection for my phone while still being slim and sleek enough to slide into my pocket easily.

The cactus leather material is a pleasant surprise. It’s not the traditional leather I’m used to on phone cases, but honestly, I like it even better! It has a unique texture and appearance that feels modern and premium. Plus, knowing it’s a more sustainable material makes it even more appealing.

The MagSafe compatibility works flawlessly, and the overall fit and finish of the case are perfect. If you’re looking for a durable, stylish, and eco-friendly case, this one is a no-brainer. Highly recommend!

reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 14, 2026

reviews.content: Love the design, 3 months of use and design is holding up. But does not protect the edges like other OtterBox cases make sure you have good glass on.

reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 28, 2026
reviews.content: Perfect fit, but would expect nothing less from Otterbox! Bought it for my wife’s phone and she loves it.

reviews.content: Looks great, fits perfectly, feels good in your hand, but it quickly showed signs of wear. The case didn’t last a year.
